findings.title: Claim-grade per-model estimates are computed on incommensurable, partly non-observational row populations
findings.target: technical/index.html Part 4 ('Eight models, one measurement', 'the same two-phrasing probe, run on eight measured open-weights models') and data/model_stats.json per_model, produced by patientwords-engine/scripts/paired_stats_rigor.py analyze()
findings.argument: analyze() filters rows only by model and holdout flag — there is no batch/provenance filter. The site's own data/urgency_shift.json shows what therefore feeds gemma-2-2b's 'claim-grade' numbers (n_rows=1020): steering-intervention rows (boostgrid_s2p5/s5/s20/lowrank, urgency_downgrades_boostgrid — steered runs voting in the majority flip label of their phrase), the outcome-selected reviewed downgrade set (urgency_downgrades, chosen because they downgraded), the hand-built imported_pairs the site elsewhere promises are 'kept apart', plus repeatability and drift-sentinel re-traces. Every other model's rows come from pairs_* batches only, and the four ~120-phrase models (llama-3.2-3b, olmo-2-1b, gemma-2-2b-it, medgemma-4b-it) were measured once on a single pre-Tier-B batch (convergence.json: n frozen at 119–122 across 12 nightly re-runs) while the 670–680-phrase models pool Tier A plus haiku-generated Tier B — despite the prereg's rule that generator 'enters as a fixed effect in pooled models', no adjustment appears anywhere. The forest plot and dumbbells invite cross-model comparison (olmo 'worst' at −7.2 pp) of estimates that differ simultaneously in model, stimulus set, generator, and data hygiene.
findings.fix: Restrict the confirmatory table to observational pairs_* rows (convergence_tracker.py already does exactly this — copy its scope filter into paired_stats_rigor.py), report the all-rows version as a sensitivity, add a generator/tier covariate or a same-phrase-subset comparison (the engine's paired_stats.py unified-set analysis) for any cross-model contrast, and annotate the four frozen-subset models as measured on one early batch.

findings.title: 'Every band sits below zero' is a joint claim over eight uncorrected, non-independent 95% CIs, with a post-hoc BH family behind the only corrected tests
findings.target: technical/index.html Part 4 ('Every band sits below zero', conv-fold summary 'every band settles below zero', dumbbell prose 'downgrades outnumber upgrades on every model') and methods.html limitations ('medgemma-4b-it ... still shows a below-zero penalty'); data/model_stats.json
findings.argument: The page corrects only the sign tests ('the asymmetry tests are adjusted for multiple comparisons') while the headline claim is a simultaneous statement about eight penalty CIs, each marginal 95%. The weakest interval, medgemma-4b-it, ends at −0.22 pp (ci95 [−0.0686, −0.0022], n=119); any simultaneity adjustment (Bonferroni over 8 needs ~99.4% intervals) would push it across zero, yet the limitations section separately leans on this model as the medically-tuned confirmation. The dumbbell headline asserts direction 'on every model' when two of eight asymmetries do not survive the correction (gemma-2-2b-it q=0.289; medgemma q=0.075). And the BH family itself is data-dependent: the prereg registered BH 'across models' when the models were the four logits models; four more were added mid-collection (divergence log, 'secondary/exploratory'), yet the published family is all eight (benjamini_hochberg.across, n_tests=8; the rigor script's docstring still says 'four times'), with confirmatory and post-hoc models visually indistinguishable in the claim-grade table.
findings.fix: When making the every-model claim, publish simultaneous intervals (max-t or Bonferroni-adjusted percentile bootstrap — one flag in paired_stats_rigor.py) and soften the sentence if medgemma fails; run BH over the pre-registered four as the confirmatory family and report the four added models in a separate exploratory block of the table; reword the medgemma limitation to 'point estimate below zero, interval nearly touching it, exploratory addition'.

findings.title: The sealed holdout cannot deliver the registered precision, and its per-pair outcomes are published nightly on the public site
findings.target: docs/preregistration_tierB.md Amendment 1; technical/index.html Part 4 ('One tenth of newly collected data is sealed in advance and never touched by these interim numbers'; endnote 'a pre-registered holdout still sealed')
findings.argument: Two independent problems. (1) Power: the prereg's own calculation says the primary downgrade-rate endpoint needs ~1,000 usable pairs for ±2%; the holdout is sha1 mod 10 ≈ 10% of Tier B — at the full 1,600-pair target that is ~160 pairs, ~77 flips at the observed ~48% flip rate, giving a Clopper-Pearson half-width around ±8 pp; at current accrual it is worse. Amendment 1 says the holdout 'is analyzed exactly once ... against the §Endpoints as written' but never states whether the confirmatory CI is computed on the holdout alone (underpowered by 4x against its own precision target) or pooled with the nightly-inspected exploration split (contaminated). (2) Sealing: urgency_shift.py --publish writes ALL rows — holdout included — to the public data/urgency_shift.json, and export_frontend_simulated.py has no tierb_split handling, so every holdout pair's flip, penalty, and downgrade badge has been rendered in the public gallery all week. The seal excludes holdout rows from aggregate files only; the analysts and owner see individual holdout outcomes daily, which is precisely the informal-look channel a garden-of-forking-paths guard exists to close.
findings.fix: Amend now, before analysis day: state the confirmatory dataset (holdout-only vs pooled-with-penalty-for-looks) and the reconciliation rule if exploration and holdout disagree; restate the achievable holdout precision honestly (±8–10 pp, not ±2%); withhold holdout rows from the published payloads until the sealed analysis lands, or change the site language from 'sealed ... never touched' to 'excluded from all published aggregates (individual measurements are public)'.

findings.title: Interim, sequentially-inspected estimates are branded 'claim-grade' and the convergence fold narrates repeated looks as terminal tests
findings.target: technical/index.html: endnote 'Part 4's cross-model statistics are the claim-grade interim numbers'; conv fold prose 'at 36 phrases the downgrade pattern in gemma-3-4b-it could still have been chance; by 155 it could not' and 'the newest additions, at about 120 phrases each, have settled below zero as well'; data/convergence.json
findings.argument: The Part 4 numbers are recomputed nightly with no alpha-spending, batch size was doubled mid-collection in view of accumulating results (divergence log, 'go 100'), and models were added after early results — yet the public label is 'claim-grade'. The convergence sentence commits the sequential-testing error explicitly: it declares chance excluded at a data-chosen interim look (n=155), where the raw sign-test p (~0.0007) carries no correction for the ~19 looks plotted — and at that very look the model's penalty CI still crossed zero ([−0.0663, +0.0054] in convergence.json), so the narration silently switches from the penalty band the figure draws to the flip asymmetry. Separately, 'settled below zero' is wrong as a description for four of the eight panels: llama, olmo, gemma-2-2b-it, and medgemma have n frozen at 119–122 across all 12 plotted points — nothing accumulated, nothing settled; it is one estimate re-plotted 12 times under a fold titled 'How confidence accumulated'.
findings.fix: Reserve 'claim-grade' for the sealed holdout analysis and relabel Part 4 'interim, exploration split'; delete or rewrite the 36/155 sentence (either descriptive — 'the point estimate stabilized' — or with a group-sequential bound); for the frozen-n models, replace 'settled below zero' with 'measured once at ~120 phrases; interval below zero' and suppress their panels from the accumulation frame.

findings.title: The 'cluster bootstrap' is an iid bootstrap over phrase means; the only dependence it removes is exact-string duplication, while the design's real clustering is above the phrase level
findings.target: model_stats.json ci_method_penalty ('cluster bootstrap (percentile 95%), cluster = clinical_prompt phrase') and the technical page's Statistical methods list ('Penalty intervals: cluster bootstrap ... cluster = phrase'); paired_stats_rigor.py cluster_bootstrap_ci()
findings.argument: cluster_bootstrap_ci() resamples values that are 'already one-per-cluster phrase means' — after dedupe every cluster is a singleton, so this is an ordinary nonparametric bootstrap wearing a cluster label. The label buys credibility for exactly one threat (verbatim re-traces), which the deterministic tracer (0/60 repeat differences) makes nearly information-free anyway. The dependence that actually threatens the ±1 pp interval on n=680 'phrases' is unmodeled: stimuli are LLM-generated in 50–100-pair batches from eight standing topic areas, anchored on seed pairs, so near-duplicate scenarios that differ by one token count as fully independent clusters; the dedupe key is exact string equality on clinical_prompt. The majority-vote rule compounds this: rows sharing a clinical_prompt but differing on the patient side (paraphrase variants — which the prereg assigns to a separate paraphrase-averaged analysis) are collapsed to one mean penalty and one voted flip label, mixing two estimands inside the primary one; the tie-break is documented and conservative, but the vote also lets non-observational rows (finding 1) flip a phrase's label.
findings.fix: Report batch-level and topic-level cluster bootstraps as pre-specified sensitivity intervals (both fields exist on collector rows) alongside a duplication diagnostic (e.g., near-duplicate rate by edit distance on clinical_prompt); restrict the majority vote to rows sharing the full (clinical, patient) pair and handle paraphrase variants via the prereg's paraphrase-averaged path; rename the CI method honestly ('phrase-level bootstrap after dedupe').

findings.title: Impossible q-values ('0'), unstated sidedness, and a stale multiplicity statement
findings.target: technical/index.html ev-table q column (renders p_bh as '0' for four models) and Statistical methods list; paired_stats_rigor.py sign_test() rounding and module docstring ('the false-discovery cost of having asked the question four times')
findings.argument: An exact sign test cannot produce p=0; sign_test() rounds to 5 decimals, so model_stats.json stores p_raw/p_bh of 0.0 and the public table prints 'q = 0' — a value no exact test can take, and one that invites over-reading in exactly the table labeled claim-grade. The prereg ('asymmetry tested by exact sign test') never fixes sidedness; the implementation is two-sided (verifiable from llama's 9–0 giving p=0.00391=2/2^9), which is fine but should be stated on the page since the hypothesis is directional. The rigor script's docstring still describes the BH family as four questions while n_tests=8, so the code's own documentation of the correction is wrong.
findings.fix: Emit exact p-values unrounded (or floor the display at '< 0.00001') in both the JSON and the ev-table renderer; add 'two-sided' to the Statistical methods bullet; update the docstring to the actual family and its confirmatory/exploratory split.
